---
title: "Assignment-1"
author: "Tushar Sharma"
Roll No:"25040208013"
---

Demonstration of different types of operators taught in Class

```{r}
# ARITHMETIC OPERATORS
# Using variables a1 and b1 for arithmetic operations

a1 <- 10
b1 <- 3

print(a1 + b1)   # Addition
print(a1 - b1)   # Subtraction
print(a1 * b1)   # Multiplication
print(a1 / b1)   # Division
print(a1 %% b1)  # Remainder
print(a1 %/% b1) # Integer division
print(a1 ^ b1)   # Power

```

```{r}
# COMPARISON (RELATIONAL) OPERATORS
# Using variables c1 and d1 for comparison operations

c1 <- 8
d1 <- 5

print(c1 > d1)   # Greater than
print(c1 < d1)   # Less than
print(c1 == d1)  # Equal to
print(c1 != d1)  # Not equal to
print(c1 >= d1)  # Greater than or equal to
print(c1 <= d1)  # Less than or equal to


```

```{r}
# LOGICAL OPERATORS
# Using logical variables l1 and l2

l1 <- TRUE
l2 <- FALSE

print(l1 & l2)   # Logical AND
print(l1 | l2)   # Logical OR
print(!l1)       # Logical NOT

```

```{r}
# ASSIGNMENT OPERATORS
# Demonstrating different assignment operators

x1 <- 5
print(x1)

x2 = 10
print(x2)

15 -> x3
print(x3)

```

```{r}
#Vector used

# Created a vector Apple that contain diffrent types of appel
apple<-c('red','green','yellow','cotton')
print(apple)

#class Shows that what kind of data is present in the particular vector
print(class(apple))     

```

```{r}
# MISCELLANEOUS OPERATORS
# Sequence operator (:)
print(1:5)

#  operator (%in%)
items <- c(2, 4, 6, 8)
print(4 %in% items)
print(8 %in% items)
```

```{r}
# VECTOR OPERATIONS
# Creating numeric vectors and performing various operations


v1 <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)
v2 <- c(10, 20, 30, 40)

# Arithmetic operations (element-wise)
print(v1 + v2)   # Vector addition
print(v1 * v2)   # Vector multiplication
print(v2 - v1)   # Vector subtraction
print(v2 / v1)   # Vector division

# Comparison operations on vectors
print(v1 > 2)    # Greater than comparison
print(v1 <= v2)  # Vector-to-vector comparison

# Mathematical functions on vectors
print(sum(v1))       # Sum of elements
print(mean(v2))      # Mean of elements
print(max(v2))       # Maximum value
print(min(v1))       # Minimum value
print(length(v1))    # Number of elements

# Indexing and subsetting vectors
print(v1[1])         # First element
print(v2[2:4])       # Elements from index 2 to 4
print(v1[-1])        # All elements except first

# Conditional subsetting
print(v2[v2 > 20])           # Elements greater than 20
print(v1[v1 %% 2 == 0])      # Even numbers from v1

# Sorting vectors
print(sort(v2))                      # Ascending order
print(sort(v2, decreasing = TRUE))   # Descending order

# Combining and repeating vectors
v3 <- c(v1, v2)
print(v3)

```

```{r}
# List is an R-object which can contain different types of elements
# like vectors, numbers, functions and even another list
# Creating first list

list_A <- list(numbers = c(10, 20, 30),name = "Tushar",passed = TRUE)

print(list_A)

# Creating second list
list_B <- list(marks = c(70, 75, 80),age = 21
)

print(list_B)

# Accessing elements from list

print(list_A[[1]])        # Access by position
print(list_A$name)        # Access by name
print(list_B$marks)

# Modifying elements in list

list_A$name <- "Tushar Sharma"
print(list_A)

list_B$age <- 22
print(list_B)

# Adding new elements to list

list_A$grade <- "A"
print(list_A)

list_B$result <- TRUE
print(list_B)

# Length of lists

print(length(list_A))
print(length(list_B))

# Combining two lists

combined_list <- c(list_A, list_B)
print(combined_list)

# List inside list (nested list)

final_list <- list(personal_details = list_A,academic_details = list_B)

print(final_list)

# Removing an element from list

list_A$passed <- NULL
print(list_A)

```

```{r}
#Matrix is a two dimensional vector

#learning how to define a matrix
M=matrix(c('a','a','b','c','b','a'),nrow=2,ncol=3,byrow=TRUE)
print(M)

#Some operation on matrices

M1=matrix(c(1,0,0,0,1,0,0,0,1),nrow=3,ncol=3,byrow=TRUE)
print(M1)

print(dim(M1))      # Dimensions (rows, columns)
print(nrow(M1))     # Number of rows
print(ncol(M1))     # Number of columns


# Accessing matrix elements
print(M1[1, 1])     # Element at row 1, column 1
print(M1[2, ])      # Entire 2nd row
print(M1[, 3])      # Entire 3rd column

# Matrix arithmetic operations

M2 <- matrix( c(2, 2, 2, 1, 1, 1,3, 3, 3),nrow = 3,ncol=3,byrow = TRUE)

print(M2)

print(M1 + M2)      # Matrix addition
print(M2 - M1)      # Matrix subtraction
print(M1 * M2)      # Element-wise multiplication

# Matrix multiplication
print(M1 %*% M2)    # Proper matrix multiplication


# Transpose of a matrix

print(t(M2))

# Diagonal, determinant and identity check

print(diag(M1))     # Diagonal elements
print(det(M1))      # Determinant of matrix
```

```{r}

```

```{r}
# FACTOR – TYPES OF SPORTS PLAYED BY STUDENTS
sports_played <- c("Football","Handball","Volleyball","Cricket","basketball ","khokho")
sport_factor <- factor(sports_played)
print(sport_factor)


```

```{r}
# SEMESTER 1 MARKS TABLE 
# Creating a structured table for Semester 1 marks

sem1_marks <- data.frame(
  Subject_Code = c("MDSC-101", "MDSC-102", "MDSC-103", "MDSC-104", "MDSC-105"),
  Subject_Name = c(
    "Applied Linear Algebra",
    "Inferential Statistics",
    "Optimization Techniques",
    "Computer Organization and Architecture",
    "Design and Analysis of Algorithms"
  ),
  Grade_Point = c(8.0, 7.0, 8.0, 8.0, 9.0)
)

print(sem1_marks)

# A simple use case of the list structure

student_record <- list(
  student_name = "Tushar Sharma",
  roll_no = 25040208013,
  semester = "Semester 1",
  marks_table = sem1_marks,
  passed = TRUE
)

print(student_record)
```
